title: UNCERTAINTY SETS FOR IMAGE CLASSIFIERS USING CONFORMAL PREDICTION [PDF]

Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

## UNCERTAINTY SETS FOR IMAGE CLASSIFIERS USING CONFORMAL PREDICTION

**Anastasios N. Angelopoulos, Stephen Bates** _[∗][∗]_ **, Jitendra Malik, & Michael I. Jordan** Departments of Electrical Engineering and Computer Sciences and Statistics University of California, Berkeley

_{_ angelopoulos,stephenbates,malik,jordan _}_ @cs.berkeley.edu

## ABSTRACT

Convolutional image classifiers can achieve high predictive accuracy, but quantifying their uncertainty remains an unresolved challenge, hindering their deployment in consequential settings. Existing uncertainty quantification techniques, such as Platt scaling, attempt to calibrate the network’s probability estimates, but they do not have formal guarantees. We present an algorithm that modifies any classifier to output a _predictive set_ containing the true label with a user-specified probability, such as 90%. The algorithm is simple and fast like Platt scaling, but provides a formal finite-sample coverage guarantee for every model and dataset. Our method modifies an existing conformal prediction algorithm to give more stable predictive sets by regularizing the small scores of unlikely classes after Platt scaling. In experiments on both Imagenet and Imagenet-V2 with ResNet-152 and other classifiers, our scheme outperforms existing approaches, achieving coverage with sets that are often factors of 5 to 10 smaller than a stand-alone Platt scaling baseline.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Imagine you are a doctor making a high-stakes medical decision based on diagnostic information from a computer vision classifier. What would you want the classifier to output in order to make the best decision? This is not a casual hypothetical; such classifiers are already used in medical settings (e.g., Razzak et al., 2018; Lundervold & Lundervold, 2019; Li et al., 2014). A maximumlikelihood diagnosis with an accompanying probability may not be the most essential piece of information. To ensure the health of the patient, you must also rule in or rule out harmful diagnoses. In other words, even if the most likely diagnosis is a stomach ache, it is equally or more important to rule out stomach cancer. Therefore, you would want the classifier to give you—in addition to an estimate of the most likely outcome—actionable uncertainty quantification, such as a set of predictions that provably covers the true diagnosis with a high probability (e.g., 90%). This is called a _prediction set_ (see Figure 1). Our paper describes a method for constructing prediction sets from any pre-trained image classifier that are formally guaranteed to contain the true class with the desired probability, relatively small, and practical to implement. Our method modifies a conformal predictor (Vovk et al., 2005) given in Romano et al. (2020) for the purpose of modern image classification in order to make it more stable in the presence of noisy small probability estimates. Just as importantly, we provide extensive evaluations and code for conformal prediction in computer vision.

Formally, for a discrete response _Y ∈Y_ = _{_ 1 _, . . . , K}_ and a feature vector _X ∈_ R _[d]_ , we desire an uncertainty set function, _C_ ( _X_ ), mapping a feature vector to a subset of _{_ 1 _, . . . , K}_ such that

for a pre-specified confidence level _α_ such as 10%. Conformal predictors like our method can modify any black-box classifier to output predictive sets that are rigorously guaranteed to satisfy the desired _coverage_ property shown in Eq. (1). For evaluations, we focus on Imagenet classification

using convolutional neural networks (CNNs) as the base classifiers, since this is a particularly challenging testbed. In this setting, _X_ would be the image and _Y_ would be the class label. Note that the guarantee in Eq. (1) is _marginal_ over _X_ and _Y_ —it holds on average, not for a particular image _X_ .

A first approach toward this goal might be to assemble the set by including classes from highest to lowest probability (e.g., after Platt scaling and a softmax function; see Platt et al., 1999; Guo et al., 2017) until their sum just exceeds the threshold 1 _− α_ . We call this strategy naive and formulate it precisely in Algorithm 1. There are two problems with naive: first, the probabilities output by CNNs are known to be incorrect (Nixon et al., 2019), so the sets from naive do not achieve coverage. Second, image classification models’ tail probabilities are often badly miscalibrated, leading to large sets that do not faithfully articulate the uncertainty of the model; see Section 2.3. Moreover, smaller sets that achieve the same coverage level can be generated with other methods.

The coverage problem can be solved by picking a new threshold using holdout samples. For example, with _α_ =10%, if choosing sets that contain 93% estimated probability achieves 90% coverage on the holdout set, we use the 93% cutoff instead. We refer to this algorithm, introduced in Romano et al. (2020), as _Adaptive Prediction Sets_ (APS). The APS procedure provides coverage but still produces large sets. To fix this, we introduce a regularization technique that tempers the influence of these noisy estimates, leading to smaller, more stable sets. We describe our proposed algorithm, _Regularized Adaptive Prediction Sets_ (RAPS), in Algorithms 2 and 3 (with APS as a special case). As we will see in Section 2, both APS and RAPS are always guaranteed to satisfy Eq. (1)—regardless of model and dataset. Furthermore, we show that RAPS is guaranteed to have better performance than choosing a fixed-size set. Both methods impose negligible computational requirements in both training and evaluation, and output useful estimates of the model’s uncertainty on a new image given, say, 1000 held-out examples.

In Section 3 we conduct the most extensive evaluation of conformal prediction in deep learning to date on Imagenet and Imagenet-V2. We find that RAPS sets always have smaller average size than naive and APSsets. For example, using a ResNeXt-101, naive does not achieve coverage, while APS and RAPS achieve it almost exactly. However, APS sets have an average size of 19, while RAPS sets have an average size of 2 at _α_ = 10% (Figure 2 and Table 1). We will provide an accompanying codebase that implements our method as a wrapper for any PyTorch classifier, along with code to exactly reproduce all of our experiments.

## 1.1 RELATED WORK

Reliably estimating predictive uncertainty for neural networks is an unsolved problem. Historically, the standard approach has been to train a Bayesian neural network to learn a distribution over network weights (Quinonero-Candela et al., 2005; MacKay, 1992; Neal, 2012; Kuleshov et al., 2018; Gal, 2016). This approach requires computational and algorithmic modifications; other approaches avoid these via ensembles (Lakshminarayanan et al., 2017; Jiang et al., 2018) or approximations of Bayesian inference (Riquelme et al., 2018; Sensoy et al., 2018). These methods also have major practical limitations; for example, ensembling requires training many copies of a neural network adversarially. Therefore, the most widely used strategy is ad-hoc _traditional calibration_ of the softmax scores with Platt scaling (Platt et al., 1999; Guo et al., 2017; Nixon et al., 2019).

This work develops a method for uncertainty quantification based on _conformal prediction_ . Originating in the online learning literature, conformal prediction is an approach for generating predictive sets that satisfy the coverage property in Eq. (1) (Vovk et al., 1999; 2005). We use a convenient data-splitting version known as _split conformal prediction_ that enables conformal prediction meth-

ods to be deployed for essentially any predictor (Papadopoulos et al., 2002; Lei et al., 2018). While mechanically very different from traditional calibration as discussed above, we will refer to our approach as _conformal calibration_ to highlight that the two methodologies have overlapping but different goals.

Conformal prediction is a general framework, not a specific algorithm—important design decisions must be made to achieve the best performance for each context. To this end, Romano et al. (2020) and Cauchois et al. (2020) introduce techniques aimed at achieving coverage that is similar across regions of feature space, whereas Vovk et al. (2003); Hechtlinger et al. (2018) and Guan & Tibshirani (2019) introduce techniques aimed at achieving equal coverage for each class. While these methods have conceptual appeal, thus far there has been limited empirical evaluation of this general approach for state-of-the-art CNNs. Concretely, the only works that we are aware of that include some evaluation of conformal methods on ImageNet—the gold standard for benchmarking computer vision methods—are Hechtlinger et al. (2018), Park et al. (2019), Cauchois et al. (2020), and Messoudi et al. (2020), although in all four cases further experiments are needed to more fully evaluate their operating characteristics for practical deployment. At the heart of conformal prediction is the _conformal score_ - a measure of similarity between labeled examples which is used to compare a new point to among those in a hold out set. Our theoretical contribution can be summarized as a modification of the conformal score from Romano et al. (2020) to have smaller, more stable sets. Lastly, there are alternative approaches to returning prediction sets not based on conformal prediction (Pearce et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2018). These methods can be used as input to a conformal procedure to potentially improve performance, but they do not have finite-sample coverage guarantees when used alone.

## 2 METHODS

In developing uncertainty set methods to improve upon naive, we are guided by three desiderata. First and most importantly, the _**coverage desideratum**_ says the sets must provide 1 _− α_ coverage, as discussed above. Secondly, the _**size desideratum**_ says we want sets of small size, since these convey more detailed information and may be more useful in practice. Lastly, the _**adaptiveness desideratum**_ says we want the sets to communicate instance-wise uncertainty: they should be smaller for easy test-time examples than for hard ones; see Figure 1 for an illustration. Coverage and size are obviously competing objectives, but size and adaptiveness are also often in tension. The size desideratum seeks small sets, while the adaptiveness desideratum seeks larger sets when the classi-

We now turn to the specifics of our proposed method. We begin in Subsection 2.1 by describing an abstract data-splitting procedure called _conformal calibration_ that enables the near-automatic construction of valid predictive sets (that is, sets satisfying Eq. (1)). Subsequently, in Subsection 2.2, we provide a detailed presentation of our procedure, with commentary in Section 2.3. In Subsection 2.4 we discuss the optimality of our procedure, proving that it is at least as good as the procedure that returns sets of a fixed size, unlike alternative approaches.

## 2.1 CONFORMAL CALIBRATION

We first review a general technique for producing valid prediction sets, following the articulation in Gupta et al. (2019). Consider a procedure that outputs a predictive set for each observation, and further suppose that this procedure has a tuning parameter _τ_ that controls the size of the sets. (In RAPS, _τ_ is the cumulative sum of the sorted, penalized classifier scores.) We take a small independent _conformal calibration set_ of data, and then choose the tuning parameter _τ_ such that the predictive sets are large enough to achieve 1 _− α_ coverage on this set. See Figure 3 for an illustration. This calibration step yields a choice of _τ_ , and the resulting set is formally guaranteed to have coverage 1 _− α_ on a future test point from the same distribution; see Theorem 1 below.

The second argument _u_ is included to allow for randomized procedures; let _U_ 1 _, . . . , Un_ be i.i.d. uniform [0 _,_ 1] random variables that will serve as the second argument for each data point. ## 2.2 OUR METHOD

Conformal calibration is a powerful general idea, allowing one to achieve the coverage desideratum for any choice of sets _C_ ( _x, u, τ_ ). Nonetheless, this is not yet a full solution, since the quality of the resulting prediction sets can vary dramatically depending on the design of _C_ ( _x, u, τ_ ). In particular, we recall the size and adaptiveness desiderata from Section 1—we want our uncertainty sets to be as small as possible while faithfully articulating the instance-wise uncertainty of each test point. In this section, we explicitly give our algorithm, which can be viewed as a special case of conformal calibration with the uncertainty sets _C_ designed to extract information from CNNs.

Our algorithm has three main ingredients. First, for a feature vector _x_ , the base model computes class probabilities _π_ ˆ _x ∈_ R _[k]_ , and we order the classes from most probable to least probable. Then, we add a regularization term to promote small predictive sets. Finally, we conformally calibrate the penalized prediction sets to guarantee coverage on future test points.

2.3 WHY REGULARIZE?

In our experiments, the sets from APS are larger than necessary, because APS is sensitive to the noisy probability estimates far down the list of classes. This noise leads to a _permutation problem_ of unlikely classes, where ordering of the classes with small probability estimates is determined mostly by random chance. If 5% of the true classes from the calibration set are deep in the tail due to the permutation problem, APS will choose large 95% predictive sets; see Figure 2. The inclusion of the RAPS regularization causes the algorithm to avoid using the unreliable probabilities in the tail; see Figure 4. We discuss how RAPS improves the adaptiveness of APS in Section 4 and Appendix E.

2.4 OPTIMALITY CONSIDERATIONS

Table 1: **Results on Imagenet-Val.** We report coverage and size of the optimal, randomized fixed sets, naive, APS, and RAPS sets for nine different Imagenet classifiers. The median-of-means for each column is reported over 100 different trials. See Section 3.1 for full details.

In this section we report on experiments that study the performance of the predictive sets from naive, APS, and RAPS, evaluating each based on the three desiderata above. We begin with a brief preview of the experiments. In **Experiment 1** , we evaluate naive, APS, and RAPS on ImagenetVal. Both APS and RAPS provided almost exact coverage, while naive sets had coverage slightly below the specified level. APS has larger sets on average than naive and RAPS. RAPS has a much smaller average set size than APS and naive. In **Experiment 2** , we repeat Experiment 1 on Imagenet-V2, and the conclusions still hold. In **Experiment 3** , we produce histograms of set sizes for naive, APS, and RAPS for several different values of _λ_ , illustrating a simple tradeoff between set size and adaptiveness. In **Experiment 4** , we compute histograms of RAPS sets stratified by image difficulty, showing that RAPS sets are smaller for easier images than for difficult ones. In **Experiment 5** , we report the performance of RAPS with many values of the tuning parameters.

In our experiments, we use nine standard, pretrained Imagenet classifiers from the torchvision repository (Paszke et al., 2019) with standard normalization, resize, and crop parameters. Before applying naive, APS, or RAPS, we calibrated the classifiers using the standard temperature scaling/Platt scaling procedure as in Guo et al. (2017) on the calibration set. Thereafter, naive, APS, and RAPS were applied, with RAPS using a data-driven choice of parameters described in Appendix E. We use the randomized versions of these algorithms—see Appendix B for a discussion.

3.1 EXPERIMENT 1: COVERAGE VS SET SIZE ON IMAGENET

In this experiment, we calculated the coverage and mean set size of each procedure for two different choices of _α_ . Over 100 trials, we randomly sampled two subsets of Imagenet-Val: one conformal calibration subset of size 20K and one evaluation subset of size 20K. The median-of-means over trials for both coverage and set size are reported in Table 1. Figure 2 illustrates the performances of naive, APS, and RAPS; RAPS has much smaller sets than both naive and APS, while achieving coverage. We also report results from a conformalized fixed-k procedure, which finds the smallest fixed set size achieving coverage on the holdout set, _k[∗]_ , then predicts sets of size _k[∗] −_ 1 or _k[∗]_ on new examples in order to achieve exact coverage; see Algorithm 4 in Appendix E.

3.2 EXPERIMENT 2: COVERAGE VS SET SIZE ON IMAGENET-V2

The same procedure as Experiment 1 was repeated on Imagenet-V2, with exactly the same normalization, resize, and crop parameters. The size of the calibration and evaluation sets was 5K, since Imagenet-V2 is a smaller dataset. The result shows that our method can still provide coverage even for models trained on different distributions, as long as the conformal calibration set comes from the new distribution. The variance of the coverage is higher due to having less data.

3.3 EXPERIMENT 3: SET SIZES OF N A I V E, APS, AND RAPS ON IMAGENET

We investigate the effect of regularization in more detail. For three values of _λ_ , we collected the set sizes produced by each of naive, APS, and RAPS and report their histograms in Figure 4.

3.4 EXPERIMENT 4: ADAPTIVENESS OF RAPS ON IMAGENET

We now show that RAPS sets are smaller for easy images than hard ones, addressing the adaptiveness desideratum. Experiments 3 and 4 together illustrate the tradeoff between adaptiveness and size: as the average set size decreases, the RAPS procedure truncates sets larger than the smallest fixed set that provides coverage, taming the heavy tail of the APS procedure. Since RAPS with large _λ_ undercovers hard examples, it must compensate by taking larger sets for easy examples to ensure the 1 _− α_ marginal coverage guarantee. However, the size only increases slightly since easy images are more common than hard ones, and the total probability mass can often exceed _τ_ ˆ _ccal_ by including only one more class. If this behavior is not desired, we can instead automatically pick _λ_ to optimize the adaptiveness of RAPS; see Section 4.

## 3.5 EXPERIMENT 5: CHOICE OF TUNING PARAMETERS

While any value of the tuning parameters _λ_ and _kreg_ lead to coverage (Proposition 1), some values will lead to smaller sets. In Experiments 1 and 2, we chose _kreg_ and _λ_ adaptively from data (see Appendix E), achieving strong results for all models and choices of the coverage level. Table 3 gives the performance of RAPS with many choices of _kreg_ and _λ_ for ResNet-152.

## 4 ADAPTIVENESS AND CONDITIONAL COVERAGE

In this section, we point to a definition of adaptiveness that is more natural for the image classification setting than the existing notion of conditional coverage. We show that APS does not satisfy conditional coverage, and that RAPS with small _λ_ outperforms it in terms of adaptiveness.

We say that a set-valued predictor _C_ : R _[d] →_ 2 _[Y]_ satisfies exact _conditional coverage_ if _P_ ( _Y ∈ C_ ( _X_ ) _| X_ = _x_ ) = 1 _− α_ for each _x_ . Distribution-free guarantees on conditional coverage are impossible (Vovk, 2012; Lei & Wasserman, 2014), but many algorithms try to satisfy it approximately (Romano et al., 2019; 2020; Cauchois et al., 2020). In a similar spirit, Tibshirani et al. (2019) suggest a notion of local conditional coverage, where one asks for coverage in a neighborhood of each point, weighted according to a chosen kernel. Cauchois et al. (2020) introduce the _worst-case slab_ metric for measuring violations of the conditional coverage property. We present a different way of measuring violations of conditional coverage.

In words, if conditional coverage holds, then coverage holds after stratifying by set size. Based on this result, In Appendix E, we introduce the _size-stratified coverage violation_ criterion, a simple and pragmatic way of quantifying adaptiveness. Then, we automatically tune _λ_ on this metric so RAPS markedly outperforms the adaptiveness of APS (see Table 8).

Table 4: **Coverage conditional on set size.** We report average coverage of images stratified by the size of the set output by RAPS using a ResNet-152 for varying _λ_ . The marginal coverage rate is 90%.

In Table 4, we report on the coverage of APS and RAPS, stratified by the size of the prediction set. Turning our attention to the _λ_ = 0 column, we see that when APS outputs a set of size 101 _−_ 1000, APS has coverage 97%, substantially higher than 90% nominal rate. By Proposition 3, we conclude that APS is not achieving exact conditional coverage, because the scores are far from the oracle probabilities. The APS procedure still achieves marginal coverage by overcovering hard examples and undercovering easy ones, an undesirable behavior. Alternatively, RAPS can be used to regularize the set sizes—for _λ_ = _._ 001 to _λ_ = _._ 01 the coverage stratified by set size is more balanced. In summary, even purely based on the adaptiveness desideratum, RAPS with light regularization is preferable to APS. Note that as the size of the training data increases, as long as _π_ ˆ is consistent, naive and APS will become more stable, and so we expect less regularization will be needed.

Lastly, we argue that conditional coverage is a poor notion of adaptiveness when the best possible model (i.e., one fit on infinite data) has high accuracy. Given such a model, the oracle procedure from Romano et al. (2020) would return the correct label with probability 1 _− α_ and the empty set with probability _α_ . That is, having correct conditional coverage for high-signal problems where _Y_ is perfectly determined by _X_ requires a perfect classifier. In our experiments on ImageNet, APS does not approximate this behavior. Therefore, conditional coverage isn’t the right goal for prediction sets with realistic sample sizes. Proposition 3 suggests a relaxation. We could require that we have the right coverage, no matter the size of the prediction set: _P_ ( _Y ∈C_ ( _X_ ) _| {|C_ ( _x_ ) _| ∈A}_ ) _≥_ 1 _− α_ for any _A ⊂{_ 0 _,_ 1 _,_ 2 _, . . . }_ ; Appendix E.2 develops this idea. We view this as a promising way to reason about adaptiveness in high-signal problems such as image classification.

## 5 DISCUSSION

For classification tasks with many possible labels, our method enables a researcher to take any base classifier and return predictive sets guaranteed to achieve a pre-specified error level, such as 90%, while retaining small average size. It is simple to deploy, so it is an attractive, automatic way to quantify the uncertainty of image classifiers—an essential task in such settings as medical diagnostics, self-driving vehicles, and flagging dangerous internet content. Predictive sets in computer vision (from RAPS and other conformal methods) have many further uses, since they systematically identify hard test-time examples. Finding such examples is useful in active learning where one only has resources to label a small number of points. In a different direction, one can improve efficiency of a classifier by using a cheap classifier outputting a prediction set first, and an expensive one only when the cheap classifier outputs a large set (a _cascade_ ; see, e.g., Li et al. 2015), and Fisch et al. (2021) for an implementation of conformal prediction in this setting. One can also use predictive sets during model development to identify failure cases and outliers and suggest strategies for improving its performance. Prediction sets are most useful for problems with many classes; returning to our initial medical motivation, we envision RAPS could be used by a doctor to automatically screen for a large number of diseases (e.g. via a blood sample) and refer the patient to relevant specialists.

progressive__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback: This paper presents a timely and technically sound contribution to the challenge of uncertainty quantification in deep learning, particularly within the high-stakes domain of image classification. The authors clearly articulate a pressing limitation of current classifiers—namely, their poorly calibrated probability outputs—and effectively motivate the need for prediction sets with formal coverage guarantees, a shift in paradigm from point-wise confidence scores to set-valued uncertainty quantification. The proposed method, Regularized Adaptive Prediction Sets (RAPS), is well-designed, building on the foundation of conformal prediction while introducing a novel regularization scheme that improves set stability and reduces size without sacrificing coverage. The clarity of exposition is strong: the problem is framed intuitively with a medical use case, formal definitions are provided with care, and the progression from naive thresholding to APS and then RAPS is logically structured. Methodologically, the paper stands out for its rare combination of rigorous theoretical guarantees—finite-sample marginal coverage under no distributional assumptions—and practical relevance, demonstrated through extensive experiments on large-scale datasets (ImageNet, ImageNet-V2) with modern architectures. However, the most significant limitation lies in the interpretation and scope of the coverage guarantee: the marginal nature of the coverage (averaged over test samples) may be insufficient in applications requiring conditional validity (e.g., per-subgroup or per-instance reliability), a point acknowledged but not deeply addressed. Additionally, while the dramatic improvements in set size (e.g., from 19 to 2 classes) are compelling, the paper could better contextualize these results—specifically, whether such small sets are consistently meaningful across classes or if performance is dominated by strong performance on easy-to-classify examples. Lastly, the related work section appears cut off, suggesting formatting errors or incomplete editing, which, if present in the original, undermines the scholarly rigor. Fixing this and elaborating on the implications of marginal vs. conditional coverage would strengthen an already impactful paper.

comments.explanation:
The authors correctly state earlier that their method (like standard conformal prediction) provides only marginal coverage over (X,Y), not conditional coverage. However, here they reference methods that aim for 'equal coverage for each class,' which could be misinterpreted as claiming class-conditional coverage guarantees. In reality, these works (e.g., Guan & Tibshirani 2019) propose methods to achieve *approximately* balanced coverage across classes via weighting or stratification, but still only in a marginal sense per class, not true conditional coverage given X.

While the statement is technically accurate — these methods do 'aim' at equal class coverage — a careful reader might infer that such methods achieve stronger guarantees than they actually do. However, the authors do not claim such guarantees themselves, and they are merely summarizing prior work. The field generally accepts that exact conditional coverage is impossible without assumptions, so this is a mild overstatement in paraphrase, not a flaw in the authors’ method. Given that this appears in a related work section explaining the landscape, and that the authors are not claiming stronger coverage for their own method, the issue is minor and likely just a shorthand.

The context resolves the concern: the authors never claim conditional or class-wise exact coverage for their method, and they are clear about the marginal nature of their guarantee. Still, the phrasing could lead some readers to believe that class-conditional coverage is achievable by existing methods, which is misleading.

comments.title: Ambiguity in definition of penalty term in RAPS
comments.quote:
Let _ox_ ( _y_ ) = _|{y[′] ∈Y_ : _π_ ˆ _x_ ( _y[′]_ ) _≥ π_ ˆ _x_ ( _y_ ) _}|_ be the ranking of _y_ among the label based on the probabilities _π_ ˆ. For example, if _y_ is the third most likely label, then _ox_ ( _y_ ) = 3.[1] We take

where ( _z_ )[+] denotes the positive part of _z_ and _λ, kreg ≥_ 0 are regularization hyperparameters...
comments.explanation:
The formula for the conformal score is defined as:

S(x, y) = ρ_x(y) + π̂_x(y) ⋅ u + λ ⋅ (o_x(y) − k_reg)_+

Here, o_x(y) is the rank of class y (1-based), and the penalty applies only when the rank exceeds k_reg. This makes sense: classes beyond the top-k_reg incur an increasing penalty, discouraging their inclusion. However, the example says: 'if y is the third most likely label, then o_x(y) = 3', which is correct.

The ambiguity lies in how the rank is used in Algorithm 2 and 3. In Algorithm 2, line 4 computes:
E_i ← Σ_{j=0}^{L_i} s[i,j] + λ ⋅ (L_i − k_reg + 1)_+

But L_i is the index of the true class in the ordered list (1-based? 0-based?). The sum is from j=0 to L_i, which suggests 0-based indexing (since j=0 is first), but L_i is defined as {j : I_{i,j} = y_i}, which likely returns the position (say, 3rd position → j=2 if 0-based). But the penalty term is (L_i − k_reg + 1)_+, which adjusts indexing to make it behave like 1-based. For example, if the true class is at position 2 (0-based), L_i = 2, then (2 − k_reg + 1) = (3 − k_reg) — this assumes the rank is 3.

Thus, the code implicitly assumes that o_x(y) = L_i + 1 (in Algorithm 2). But the mathematical definition uses o_x(y) directly as the count (≥), which for distinct scores equals the 1-based rank. So the math and code are consistent if L_i is 0-based and the rank is L_i + 1. However, this indexing shift is not explicitly stated. A reader implementing from scratch might use o_x(y) = L_i (0-based) and apply penalty too early. This is a real ambiguity in method specification.

The issue is not fatal — the penalty only affects regularization, not coverage — and the results are robust to parameter choice. But for reproducibility, the 0-based vs 1-based indexing in algorithms should be clarified. Given that this affects how hyperparameters k_reg and λ are interpreted, it’s a moderate underspecification.

comments.title: Misleading reference to Figure 2 before it is introduced
comments.quote: If 5% of the true classes from the calibration set are deep in the tail due to the permutation problem, APS will choose large 95% predictive sets; see Figure 2.
comments.explanation:
This sentence appears in Section 2.3, which is before the full presentation of the experiments in Section 3. The reference to Figure 2 occurs in the middle of methodological development, but Figure 2 is not described until after Section 2. While common in academic writing to cross-reference figures, it can disrupt flow. However, this is not a real issue — figures are often referenced before their detailed explanation.

More importantly, the claim that 'APS will choose large 95% predictive sets' if true labels fall in the tail is correct in spirit: because APS includes classes until cumulative probability exceeds 1−α, if the true class has a small estimated probability (is ranked low), then many classes must be included to cover it, inflating set size. Regularization in RAPS discourages this by penalizing late additions.

The reference to Figure 2 is appropriate — it shows that APS indeed has large average set sizes (~19) compared to RAPS (~2) at α=0.1. So the claim is supported. The forward reference is acceptable and common. No actual error — just a presentational choice.

comments.title: Incomplete equation reference in Theorem 1 and Propositions
comments.quote:
To find a function that will achieve 1 _− α_ coverage on test data, we select the smallest _τ_ that gives at least 1 _− α_ coverage on the conformal calibration set, with a slight correction to account for the finite sample size:

The set function _C_ ( _x, u, τ_ ) with this data-driven choice of _τ_ is guaranteed to have correct finite-sample coverage on a fresh test observation, as stated formally next.

    [section 2] **Theorem 1** (Conformal calibration coverage guarantee) **.** _Suppose_ ( _Xi, Yi, Ui_ ) _i_ =1 _,...,n and_ ( _Xn_ +1 _, Yn_ +1 _, Un_ +1) _are i.i.d. and let C_ ( _x, u, τ_ ) _be a set-valued function satisfying the nesting property in Eq. (2). Suppose further that the sets C_ ( _x, u, τ_ ) _grow to include all labels for large enough_ ˆ _τ : for all x ∈_ R _[d] , C_ ( _x, u, τ_ ) = _Y for some τ . Then for τ_ ccal _defined as in Eq. (3), we have the following coverage guarantee:_
comments.explanation:
The text refers to 'Eq. (2)' for nesting and 'Eq. (3)' for τ_ccal, but these equations are not displayed in the provided passage — they are forward-referenced. This is common in papers, and the definitions are standard:

- Eq. (2): nesting C(x,u,τ₁) ⊆ C(x,u,τ₂) for τ₁ ≤ τ₂
- Eq. (3): τ̂_ccal = quantile of nonconformity scores (e.g., the ⌈(n+1)(1−α)⌉-largest)

The lack of display is not an issue because the paper context assumes these are defined earlier or in standard form. Theorem 1 is a standard split conformal result (Papadopoulos et al., 2002; Vovk et al., 2005), so the claim is sound. The coverage guarantee P(Y_{n+1} ∈ C(X_{n+1}, U_{n+1}, τ̂_ccal)) ≥ 1−α holds under exchangeability.

Since this is a structural convention — the equations are presumably shown earlier — and the authors accurately describe τ_ccal in Algorithm 2 via order statistics, the missing equations are not a real problem. A reader could still verify correctness from context. Thus, no actual error, just a formatting artifact of the excerpt.

comments.title: Inconsistent use of \( \hat{\pi}_x(y) \) and \( s \) without explicit linkage
comments.quote: let \( \pi \hat{ }_x \in \mathbb{R}^{[k]} \), and we order the classes from most probable to least probable. ... formally, let \( \rho_x(y) = \sum_{y^\prime} \hat{\pi}_x(y^\prime) \mathbf{1}\{\hat{\pi}_x(y^\prime) > \hat{\pi}_x(y)\} \)
comments.explanation: The passage defines \( \rho_x(y) \) in terms of \( \hat{\pi}_x \), the class probabilities output by the model. However, in Algorithm 2 and 3, the inputs are described as sorted scores \( s \) and the associated permutation \( I \), not \( \hat{\pi}_x \). While it is natural to assume \( s \) corresponds to the sorted values of \( \hat{\pi}_x \), this identification is not explicitly stated. The prose never confirms that \( s_{i,j} = \hat{\pi}_{X_i}(I_{i,j}) \), nor does it define how the sorting is done. This creates ambiguity: is \( s \) the softmax output, logits, or some other score? In the context of conformal prediction, the choice of score can affect coverage and set size. Although the connection is likely intended and standard in the field, the absence of an explicit definition means a reader cannot fully reproduce the method without making assumptions. Given that this is a method section and reproducibility is a key concern, this omission is problematic.

comments.title: Mismatch between \( \tau_{ccal} \) definition and Algorithm 2 due to OCR-like bracket errors
comments.explanation: The expression for \( E_i \) in Algorithm 2 line 4 appears corrupted by OCR errors. The intended formula, based on the prose and Equation (4), should be: \( E_i = \sum_{j=0}^{L_i} s_{i,j} + \lambda (L_i - k_{\text{reg}} + 1)_+ \), where \( L_i \) is the position of the true class in the ordered list for example \( i \). The current text has malformed subscripts and misplaced brackets, such as [L][i][−][k][reg][+] which likely should be (Li - kreg + 1)_+. While the surrounding context (prose, Figure 3, comparison to RAPS) strongly suggests the correct formula, the OCR corruption renders the algorithm unreadable without inference. A careful reader must reconstruct \( E_i = \text{cumulative score up to true class} + \text{penalty} \). Since Algorithm 2 is a core contribution and must be executable, even plausible OCR errors that affect reproduction are significant. The severity is reduced because the correct form can be confidently inferred from multiple cross-references in the paper.

comments.title: Missing definition of \( C^*(x, u, \tau) \) before use in Proposition 1
comments.quote: _are i.i.d. and let_ **Proposition 1** (RAPS _C[∗]_ ( _x, u, τ_ coverage guarantee)) _be defined as in Eq._ **.** _Suppose(4)._
comments.explanation: Proposition 1 references a function \( C^*(x, u, \tau) \) that has not been defined in the passage. The equation label (4) refers to the definition introduced in the subsequent prose: \( C(x,u,\tau) = \{ y : \rho_x(y) + \pi_x(y) \cdot u + \lambda(o_x(y) - k_{reg})_+ \leq \tau \} \), but the notation \( C^* \) is never explained. Given that \( C(x,u,\tau) \) is already defined, the use of \( C^* \) introduces confusion. It could imply a modified version, an optimal version, or simply a typo/OCR error where the asterisk is extraneous. Since the coverage guarantee is stated immediately after and references Eq. (4)—which defines \( C \), not \( C^* \)—it is plausible that \( C^* \) is a notation error. However, without clarification, a reader might think a distinct set function is being invoked. This is a minor issue because the surrounding context clearly ties the proposition to the equation just introduced, and the guarantee follows from Theorem 1 applied to that set function.

comments.title: Ambiguous summation indices in \( \rho_x(y) \) definition with OCR artifacts
comments.quote: let \( \rho_x(y) = [\textbackslash{}sum] [K] y[\prime] =1 [\pi][\hat{}] [x][(] [y][\prime]_) [I] [{][\pi][\hat{}] x[(] [y][\prime]_) [>][\pi][\hat{}] x[(] [y]_) [}]_ [ be the total probability mass
comments.explanation: The definition of \( \rho_x(y) \) contains OCR artifacts that obscure the summation bounds and indicator function. The intended expression is clearly \( \rho_x(y) = \sum_{y' \in \mathcal{Y}} \hat{\pi}_x(y') \mathbf{1}\{ \hat{\pi}_x(y') > \hat{\pi}_x(y) \} \), but the current text has misplaced brackets and broken LaTeX. For instance, '[I] [{]' likely represents the indicator function \( \mathbf{1} \), and '[\sum][K] y[\prime]=1' should be \( \sum_{y'=1}^K \). While the prose that follows correctly describes it as 'the total probability mass of the set of labels that are more likely than \( y \)', the formula itself is unreadable due to formatting. This is an issue because formulas are meant to be precise. However, the meaning is recoverable from context, and the prose fully explains the intent. The authors correctly define the conceptual role of \( \rho_x(y) \) as a cumulative mass before \( y \), so the core idea is preserved.

comments.title: Incorrect or ambiguous set construction in Algorithm 3 line 2
comments.explanation: Line 2 of Algorithm 3 attempts to define \( L \) as the size of the prediction set. However, the expression is malformed due to OCR issues and potential logical errors. It reads like: \( L \leftarrow \left| \left\{ j \in \mathcal{Y} : \sum_{i=0}^{j} s_i + \lambda(L - k_{\text{reg}})_+ \leq \hat{\tau}_{\text{ccal}} \right\} \right| + 1 \). This is problematic because \( L \) appears both as the output cardinality and inside the set condition, creating a circular definition. The intended logic is likely: find the largest \( \ell \) such that the penalized cumulative score up to the \( \ell \)-th class is ≤ \( \hat{\tau}_{\text{ccal}} \), then set \( L = \ell \). But here, \( L \) is used in the sum before it is defined. The OCR artifacts (e.g., [L] possibly being \( \ell \)) do not resolve the circularity. The correct implementation should use a separate variable for the cumulative sum. While the randomization step (line 3–5) suggests \( L \) is meant to be an integer index, the definition in line 2 is mathematically ill-formed. This could mislead a reader implementing the algorithm, though the surrounding context and comparison to Algorithm 1 help infer the intended step-by-step construction.

comments.title: Incoherent statement in Proposition 2 due to OCR and syntax errors
comments.quote: Let k[∗] be the smallest k such that the top-k predictive sets have coverage at least (4) with any⌈_ ( _n_ + 1)(1 _− kαreg_ ) _⌉/n≤ on the conformal calibration pointsk[∗] and λ_ = 1 _. Then with τ_ ˆccal _chosen as in Eq._ ( _Xi, Yi_ ) _i_ =1 _(3), we have,...,n. Take C[∗]_ ( _x, u, τ_ ) _as in Eq._
comments.explanation: The passage containing Proposition 2 is severely corrupted, likely due to OCR errors or PDF extraction failure. The sentence structure is broken and nonsensical: 'coverage at least (4)' refers to an equation number rather than a value; 'with any⌈_ ( _n_ + 1)(1 _− kαreg_ ) _⌉/n≤ on the conformal calibration pointsk[∗]' mixes mathematical notation with prose incoherently; the phrase 'Take C[∗]_ ( _x, u, τ_ ) _as in Eq._' is cut off. Even after attempting to reconstruct, the logical connective between k∗ and the rest of the claim is missing. The intended statement seems to be that when λ = 1 and k_reg is chosen appropriately, RAPS with τ_hat_ccal achieves coverage at least 1−α and has average size no larger than k∗, the smallest fixed size achieving coverage. However, this claim is neither properly stated nor formally expressed here. The context from the surrounding discussion suggests Proposition 2 should state that RAPS dominates fixed-size sets in efficiency under correct regularization, but the current rendering renders this unintelligible. This is not merely a typesetting issue—it obliterates the mathematical content of the proposition, making it impossible to verify the authors' claim or reproduce the logic.

comments.title: Missing quantifier and logical structure in Proposition 2
comments.quote: Let k[∗] be the smallest k such that the top-k predictive sets have coverage at least (4) with any⌈_ ( _n_ + 1)(1 _− kαreg_ ) _⌉/n≤ on the conformal calibration pointsk[∗] and λ_ = 1 _.
comments.explanation: Even assuming the most plausible reconstruction—interpreting 'coverage at least (4)' as 'target coverage 1−α' and '⌈(n+1)(1−α)⌉/n' as the finite-sample correction used in conformal calibration—the sentence remains logically malformed. The phrase 'on the conformal calibration pointsk[∗]' appears to incorrectly place k∗ as a condition on the data rather than a function of it. Moreover, the dependence between k∗ and the parameters λ and k_reg is not specified. Properly, k∗ should be defined as the minimal integer k such that the empirical coverage of top-k sets on the calibration set meets or exceeds the adjusted level (e.g., ⌈(1−α)(n+1)⌉ / n). But the current text attempts to impose this threshold condition directly within the definition, while simultaneously asserting parameter settings (kreg, λ=1), creating circular logic. The paper elsewhere correctly uses the quantile-based threshold (Eq. 3), but here the synthesis of concepts is broken. The authors correctly introduce k∗ as a benchmark in the main text and intend a valid comparison, but the formal statement fails to reflect this.

comments.title: Mismatch between text description and RAPS predictive set algorithm in Algorithm 3
comments.explanation: The issue lies in the definition of L in Algorithm 3, line 2. The regularization term λ · (L − k_reg)_+ depends on L itself, which is the quantity being defined. This creates a circular dependency: L is the size of the set, but appears inside the sum condition. In contrast, the theoretical definition in Eq. (4) in the paper summary uses o_x(y), the rank of label y, which is independent of the set size. The correct formulation should sum over labels j (in order), and for each, include it if ρ_x(j) + π̂_x(j)·u + λ·(o_x(j) − k_reg)_+ ≤ τ. But here, the algorithm attempts to define the set size implicitly via a condition that references L. While this could be interpreted as a fixed-point equation (solve for smallest L such that the cumulative sum up to L satisfies the inequality), it is not standard, and the current rendering lacks a loop or iterative procedure. The paper’s correct theoretical formulation separates ordering from regularization via rank, but Algorithm 3 appears to conflate the two. Given that the OCR has rendered subscripts poorly (e.g., s[i] instead of s_i), it is possible the intended expression was Σ_{i=0}^{j-1} s_i + λ·(j − k_reg)_+ ≤ τ̂_ccal, with j indexing position. But as written, it is not executable pseudocode. This affects reproducibility and contradicts the earlier formal definition.

comments.title: Ambiguous randomization in RAPS score (Algorithm 2) due to OCR
comments.quote: 7: _Ei ← Ei − si,Li_ + _U ∗ si,Li_
comments.explanation: The randomization step in Algorithm 2 line 7 appears to read: Ei ← Ei − si,Li + U ∗ si,Li. Given the paper’s discussion of randomization in Appendix B and the purpose of conformal randomization, this should likely be Ei ← Ei − si,Li + U · si,Li, meaning that the full score si,Li is replaced with a scaled version U · si,Li where U ~ Uniform(0,1), thus randomizing the contribution of the true label’s score to the total score. This matches standard practices in conformal prediction (e.g., Romano et al., 2020) to ensure uniformity of p-values under discrete scores. However, the use of '*' could be a multiplication symbol corrupted from '·' or could misleadingly suggest a convolution or other operation. Moreover, the variable U is defined inside the conditional (line 6) but used in line 7; this is acceptable if scoping is assumed, but unclear in pseudocode. The paper’s intent is correct—randomize to ensure exact coverage—but the OCR and notation choices make the implementation ambiguous. This is unlikely to mislead an expert reader, but could affect reproducibility for a novice.

comments.title: Ambiguous interpretation of size-stratified coverage in Table 4
comments.explanation: At first glance, the table header is confusing: the column labeled 'λ = 0' corresponds to APS (as stated in the text), but is not explicitly named as such. The structure of the table—with 'cnt' and 'cvg' interleaved vertically—requires careful parsing. Initially, I was uncertain whether each pair of rows (e.g., '11627' and '0.88') corresponds to the same size bin across λ values, or if counts and coverages are misaligned. After checking the context, particularly the explanation in Section 4 that λ=0 corresponds to APS and that the table shows coverage conditional on set size, it becomes clear that each cell contains two lines: count (number of test points with that set size) and coverage (empirical coverage among those points). However, the formatting makes it easy to misread—for instance, in the λ=0.1 column, the last entry is '22\n0.59\n0', which likely means 22 instances in the 11–100 bin with coverage 0.59, and 0 in the 101–1000 bin. But this is ambiguous: does the 0.59 apply to the 22 or to a missing group? The presence of '0' below suggests no instances in the top bin, but the placement of numbers risks misalignment. While context resolves the general intent, the layout could mislead a reader into thinking that coverage drops drastically for larger sets without noticing the vanishing counts. This presentation obscures the fact that poor coverage at high λ is due to extremely few samples in large sets, not a fundamental failure. A stacked format or separate count/coverage tables would improve clarity.

comments.title: Inconsistent use of kreg in RAPS algorithm and experimental reporting
comments.quote: clips sets to be a maximum of size 5.
comments.explanation: The text states that RAPS 'clips sets to be a maximum of size 5', implying a hard cap on set size at 5. However, this contradicts both the algorithm description and Table 3. In Algorithm 3, there is no such clipping: the regularization term λ · (L − kreg)+ penalizes large sets but does not cap them. Moreover, Table 3 shows RAPS set sizes up to 11.2 (for kreg=1, λ=0), far exceeding 5. The earlier FULL PAPER CONTEXT notes that kreg=5 was used in RAPS experiments (from Table 4), which aligns with the idea that kreg=5 introduces regularization starting at the 6th class, but not that it enforces a maximum size. It appears the word 'clips' is being used informally—perhaps to suggest that regularization suppresses inclusion beyond kreg—but this is misleading. 'Clipping' typically implies a hard truncation, not a soft penalty. The authors likely mean that kreg = 5 acts as a 'regularization threshold', not a size limit. This miswording may lead a reader to incorrectly assume RAPS truncates sets at size 5, which would invalidate the coverage guarantee unless re-calibrated. The issue is resolved upon cross-checking with the algorithm and tables, but the phrasing remains problematic.

comments.title: Misleading description of RAPS behavior with large λ
comments.quote: Since RAPS with large _λ_ undercovers hard examples, it must compensate by taking larger sets for easy examples to ensure the 1 _− α_ marginal coverage guarantee.
comments.explanation: This sentence suggests that when λ is large, RAPS actively compensates for undercoverage on hard examples by inflating sets on easy ones. However, this misrepresents how conformal prediction works. The calibration threshold τ̂_ccal is chosen *once* on the calibration set to ensure marginal coverage; it does not adapt per example or adjust based on difficulty. With large λ, RAPS may struggle to cover hard examples (because regularization prevents inclusion of tail classes), leading to undercoverage *even after calibration*—but the method does *not* respond by increasing set sizes on easy examples. Instead, marginal coverage may drop below 1−α if regularization is too strong, as seen in Table 4 (e.g., λ=0.1 and λ=1 show coverage <90% in some size bins, and the text notes coverage can fall below nominal). The statement implies a feedback mechanism that does not exist: RAPS does not 'compensate'—it either satisfies marginal coverage or it doesn’t, depending on whether τ̂_ccal suffices given the regularized scoring function. The claim overstates adaptiveness and suggests dynamic adjustment that violates the static nature of conformal calibration. This could mislead readers about the method's behavior, especially regarding tradeoffs between adaptiveness and coverage.

comments.title: Potential OCR artifact in penalized score formula
comments.explanation: The notation 'y[′]' appears in multiple places (e.g., 'y[′]', '[s][i,j]'), which suggests OCR corruption where subscript brackets were misread. In the expression for ox(y), 'y[′]' is clearly meant to be y′—a dummy index for labels. This is consistent with standard mathematical notation and surrounding text (e.g., 'y′ ∈ Y'). The meaning is preserved, and the formula is otherwise correct: ox(y) is the rank of class y (counting ties conservatively via ≥). No substantive error remains once corrected, so this is not a true author error—just a PDF/OCR artifact. Similar bracket artifacts appear in sums like '[] [L] i=0', which should be '∑_{i=0}^L'. Since the context resolves the issue and the intended meaning is clear, no flag is needed beyond noting the OCR noise.

comments.title: Table 3 labels λ=0 case as RAPS despite matching APS definition
comments.explanation: The table presents results for λ=0 under various kreg values, including kreg=1,2,5,10,50. However, when λ=0, the regularization term vanishes, so the RAPS score reduces to ρx(y) + π̂x(y)·u, which is exactly the APS scoring rule (Romano et al., 2020). Thus, for λ=0, RAPS *is* APS, regardless of kreg. Yet the table suggests that kreg still plays a role, which is false—the (ox(y) − kreg)+ term is multiplied by λ=0, so it has no effect. Therefore, all rows in the λ=0 column should have the same value (11.2), not varying with kreg. The variation implies that kreg affects results even when λ=0, which contradicts the algorithm. This is likely a data or presentation error: perhaps the λ=0 results were computed with λ set to a small positive value (e.g., 1e-5), or the kreg variation is spurious. This undermines trust in the parameter sensitivity analysis. While RAPS with λ>0 does depend on kreg, in the λ=0 case it should not. The issue is not resolved by context and represents a real inconsistency.

comments.title: Inconsistency in calibration set size condition between Proposition 2 and paper summary
comments.quote: Suppose \(\lceil (n+1)(1 - \alpha) \rceil / n \leq k^*\): sufficient calibration sample size relative to desired coverage and \(k^*\)
comments.explanation: The paper summary states that the condition $\lceil (n+1)(1 - \alpha) \rceil / n \leq k^*$ is assumed, which involves $k^*$, the minimal fixed set size achieving coverage. However, in Proposition 2, this condition appears to be used as a sufficient condition for RAPS to dominate top-$k$ sets. The left-hand side $\lceil (n+1)(1 - \alpha) \rceil / n$ approximates $1 - \alpha$ for large $n$, so the condition essentially requires that $1 - \alpha \lesssim k^*$. But $k^*$ is an integer (set size), while the left-hand side is a fraction between 0 and 1. This suggests a type mismatch: comparing a continuous proportion to an integer set size. It's likely the intended condition is $\lceil (n+1)(1 - \alpha) \rceil \leq k^* n$, or more plausibly, that $k^*$ is defined such that top-$k^*$ achieves at least $1 - \alpha$ marginal coverage, and the calibration ensures $\tau_{\text{ccal}}$ selects sets of expected size near $k^*$. Given that $k^*$ is used as a scalar in context (e.g., minimum $k$ such that top-$k$ achieves coverage), the condition as written seems dimensionally inconsistent. While this may be a notational oversight—perhaps the authors meant $\lceil (n+1)(1 - \alpha) \rceil \leq n \cdot k^* / K$ or that $k^*$ is normalized—it remains ambiguous what the mathematical role of $k^*$ is in the inequality. Context from Proposition 2 and surrounding discussion does not resolve this, as the proposition states dominance under this condition without clarifying units. This raises concern about the feasibility of the assumption in practice.

comments.title: Ambiguity in definition of $ o_x(y) $ and potential off-by-one in regularization term
comments.quote: let $ o_x(y) = |\{y' \in Y : \hat{\pi}_x(y') \geq \hat{\pi}_x(y)\}| $ be the ranking of $ y $ among the label based on the probabilities $\hat{\pi}$. For example, if $ y $ is the third most likely label, then $ o_x(y) = 3 $.
comments.explanation: The definition of $o_x(y)$ counts the number of labels with probability at least $\hat{\pi}_x(y)$, so for the third most likely label, assuming no ties, it indeed includes itself and two higher ones, giving 3—this is correct. However, in Equation (4), the regularization term is $ \lambda \cdot (o_x(y) - k_{\text{reg}})_+ $. Then, in Algorithm 2, line 4 computes the score as $ \sum_{j=0}^{L_i} s_{i,j} + \lambda (L_i - k_{\text{reg}} + 1)_+ $, where $L_i$ is the index (starting from 0 or 1?) of the true label in the ordered list. There's a discrepancy: if $L_i$ corresponds to the rank (e.g., position in the permutation), and if ranks start at 1, then $L_i = o_x(y)$, so the term should be $\lambda (L_i - k_{\text{reg}})_+$. But the algorithm uses $\lambda (L_i - k_{\text{reg}} + 1)_+$. This suggests either an off-by-one error or a mismatch between the mathematical definition and implementation. For instance, if $k_{\text{reg}} = 5$, then the 6th element (rank 6) should get a penalty when $o_x(y) > 5$, i.e., $ (6 - 5)_+ = 1 $; but in the algorithm, with $L_i = 6$, we get $ (6 - 5 + 1)_+ = 2 $, applying double the penalty. Alternatively, if $L_i$ is zero-indexed (so 5th element has $L_i=4$), then $ (4 - 5 + 1)_+ = 0 $, which defers penalty to the 6th element only when $L_i = 6$ gives $ (6 - 5 + 1)_+ = 2 $. This still misaligns: the penalty should activate at rank $k_{\text{reg}} + 1$, but the implementation depends on indexing. Given that the authors state $k_{\text{reg}} = 5$ in Table 4 and discuss clipping at size 5, we expect regularization to penalize sizes beyond 5. But the current formulation may either delay or amplify the penalty incorrectly. The example in the text says ‘the sixth most likely value… has an extra penalty of size $\lambda$’, implying unit penalty at rank 6. However, the code applies $\lambda \times (6 - 5 + 1) = 2\lambda$ if $L_i = 6$. This is only resolved if $L_i$ is the zero-based index of the class in the list, and the true label is at position 5 for rank 6—unlikely. Alternatively, OCR may have corrupted the index: ‘[L][i][ −][k][reg]_[ + 1]’ might have been intended as ‘[L][i] − kreg]’, and ‘+1’ belongs to the sum bounds. But even then, the sum in line 4 starts from j=0 to Li — ambiguous whether inclusive. Given that reproducibility is emphasized, this indexing ambiguity is a moderate issue.

comments.title: Missing clarification on randomized decision rule in RAPS Algorithm 3
comments.explanation:
In Algorithm 3, line 3 computes a value $ V $ used in the randomization step, but the expression is poorly OCR'd. As written: ‘Σ _[L] i_ =0 _[−]_[1]’ likely means $ \sum_{i=0}^{L-1} s[i] $, the cumulative score up to index $L-1$. Then subtract $\lambda (L - k_{\text{reg}})_+$, add back $s[L-1]$? That would double-count $s[L-1]$. More plausibly, the expression is meant to be:  
$ V = \frac{\hat{\tau}_{\text{ccal}} - \left( \sum_{i=0}^{L-1} s[i] + \lambda (L - k_{\text{reg}})_+ \right)}{s[L-1]} $  
This matches standard conformalization randomization: $V$ is the fraction of the way $\hat{\tau}_{\text{ccal}}$ exceeds the cumulative score at $L-1$, normalized by the size of the $L$th score. But as OCR'd, it reads ‘− s[i] − λ(…) + s[L−1]’, which could suggest subtracting $s[i]$ and adding $s[L−1]$, but $i$ is index. This is clearly a formatting/OCR error. However, even assuming the correct formula, the algorithm uses $L$ as defined in line 2 which already includes the penalty term in the sum. Then in line 3, it computes $V$ using $\sum_{i=0}^{L-1} s[i] + \lambda (L - k_{\text{reg}})_+ $? But if $L$ is defined as the smallest such that the penalized sum exceeds $\hat{\tau}_{\text{ccal}}$, then $V$ should measure how far the non-penalized score would go. The standard randomization subtracts the entire excess beyond $L-1$ and sees whether to include the $L$th element. The current structure seems correct in intent: compute how much of the last score is needed to reach $\hat{\tau}_{\text{ccal}}$, then flip a coin weighted by that. But the presence of the regularization term $\lambda (L - k_{\text{reg}})_+$ in both the decision and the randomization raises a concern: should the randomization depend on the penalty? In Romano et al. (2020), randomization depends only on the base score, not penalties. Including $\lambda (L - k_{\text{reg}})_+$ in the subtraction may distort the coverage guarantee, because the randomized tie-breaking should depend only on the conformity score structure. Given that Proposition 1 cites Theorem 1, which assumes nesting and randomization via $u$, the current formulation may violate the nesting if the penalty term is discontinuous. However, since the penalty is deterministic and monotonic in $L$, nesting still holds. Still, the randomization should isolate the discontinuous jump due to $s[L]$, not the penalty. The algorithm as written ties both together. This could lead to improper randomization—e.g., if $\tau$ is just below a penalty threshold, the coin flip may depend on an artificial gap. This is a subtle but real concern affecting finite-sample calibration. The issue is not fatal, but underspecified: the paper should clarify whether randomization includes the penalty term or not.

comments.title: Ambiguity in definition of $ \rho_x(y) $ due to OCR errors and potential logical error
comments.quote: Formally, let $ \rho_x(y) =[] _[K] y[′]_ =1 _[π]_[ˆ] _[x]_[(] _[y][′]_[)][I] _[{][π]_[ˆ] _x_[(] _[y][′]_[)] _[>][π]_[ˆ] _x_[(] _[y]_[)] _[}]_[ be the total probability mass of the set of labels] that are more likely than $ y $.
comments.explanation:
The definition of $\rho_x(y)$ suffers from OCR corruption. The expression appears to be attempting:  
$ \rho_x(y) = \sum_{y' \in Y} \hat{\pi}_x(y') \cdot \mathbf{1}\{ \hat{\pi}_x(y') > \hat{\pi}_x(y) \} $  
But the OCR inserts ‘[]’ and breaks the indicator. More critically, the text says ‘total probability mass of the set of labels that are more likely than $y$’, which by strict inequality excludes $y$ itself. This is correct—$\rho_x(y)$ is the cumulative mass before $y$ in the sorted list. However, in standard conformal prediction (e.g., Romano et al., 2020), the conformity score often includes $\rho_x(y) + \pi_x(y)\cdot u$, so that randomization splits the inclusion of $y$ based on how much of its score is needed. The use of strict inequality is correct. But the definition as OCR'd is nearly unreadable and could mislead a reader into thinking it includes $y$. Additionally, the sum is over $y'=1$ to $K$, but labels are classes—this is notation abuse unless $Y = \{1,\dots,K\}$, which is acceptable. The greater concern is that $\rho_x(y)$ is only defined when the probabilities are distinct. The footnote says ties are broken randomly, which is fine. But given that modern CNNs often produce tied scores (or indistinguishable in floating point), the lack of explicit tie-breaking in the formula could affect reproducibility. This is minor given the footnote, but the OCR damage makes the formula unusable without external knowledge.

comments.title: Inconsistency between claim of RAPS outperforming fixed top-k and required assumptions
comments.quote: RAPS with proper regularization ($ \lambda = 1 $) dominates fixed top-$ k $ procedures by achieving same or smaller average set size while maintaining coverage
comments.explanation: The claim that RAPS dominates fixed top-$k$ procedures is supported in Proposition 2, but only under a condition involving $k^*$ and calibration set size. The paper suggests $\lambda = 1$ is sufficient for dominance, but Proposition 2 likely depends on $\lambda$ being tuned appropriately and $k_{\text{reg}}$ being chosen relative to the difficulty of the problem. The claim appears to generalize from a specific setting (e.g., ImageNet with ResNeXt-101) to a universal one. While the experiments support this for the models tested, the theoretical result is not unconditional. Moreover, dominance over top-$k^*$ sets assumes that $k^*$ achieves coverage—RAPS could produce smaller sets only if the model’s uncertainty estimates are informative. In low-signal regimes, RAPS may not outperform top-$k$, especially if regularization is too strong and removes relevant labels. The claim risks overgeneralization. However, the authors do qualify that this occurs ‘with proper regularization’ and show empirical support, so it's not unsubstantiated. Still, a reader might infer RAPS always dominates regardless of model quality, which is not proven. This is a mild overclaim, but resolvable from context.

comments.title: Underspecification of how $ \hat{\pi}_x $ is computed and calibrated
comments.quote: For a feature vector $ x $, the base model computes class probabilities $ \hat{\pi}_x \in \mathbb{R}^K $, and we order the classes from most probable to least probable.
comments.explanation: The paper states that temperature scaling is applied for calibration (per assumptions), but this is mentioned only in the summary, not in the main algorithm description. The core Algorithms 2 and 3 take ‘sorted scores’ and permutations as input, not raw logits. The critical preprocessing step—applying temperature scaling to the neural network's softmax outputs to calibrate $ \hat{\pi}_x $—is not described in the algorithm flow. While mentioned in Section 3.1 (experiments), it is essential for coverage and should be part of the method description. Omitting it from Algorithms 2 and 3 makes the method unreproducible: a reader might plug in uncalibrated softmax scores and fail to achieve coverage. This is a common pitfall in uncertainty quantification. The nesting property and coverage guarantee rely on proper calibration of the base probabilities; otherwise, even conformal calibration can degrade. Since the method is presented as a wrapper for any PyTorch classifier, the omission of this preprocessing step — critical for performance — constitutes an underspecification. This is particularly important because the paper contrasts its results with uncalibrated baselines (naive). While experts might assume calibration is needed, the paper’s claim of automatic coverage hinges on this step. Including it in the algorithm as a first stage would improve clarity.

progressive__glm-4.6.label: Progressive (glm-4.6)
progressive__glm-4.6.overall_feedback: This paper is exceptionally well-motivated and clearly written, effectively articulating the critical need for formal uncertainty quantification in high-stakes vision tasks through a compelling narrative. The authors successfully position their proposed method, RAPS, as a practical and theoretically-grounded improvement over existing conformal prediction techniques, promising significant empirical gains in set size while maintaining formal coverage guarantees. The clarity of the introduction is a major strength, making the core problem and the proposed solution accessible. The most significant issue for the full paper to address will be the well-known gap between the provided *marginal* coverage guarantee (which holds on average over the data distribution) and the implicit desire for *conditional* validity in the motivating examples (e.g., a doctor needs a guarantee that holds for a specific patient's symptoms, not just on average). While the introduction rightly notes this limitation, the paper's contribution could be substantially strengthened by a deeper analysis of this issue. Furthermore, while the introduction clearly states that RAPS regularizes noisy tail probabilities, the specific nature, tuning, and theoretical justification of this regularization will be crucial to evaluate the method's novelty and robustness beyond being an effective heuristic. Overall, the paper makes a strong initial case for its importance, but its ultimate impact will hinge on

comments.title: Unexplained restriction in Proposition 1
comments.quote: Suppose further that πˆx(y) > 0 for all x and y.
comments.explanation: In Proposition 1, the authors add the restriction that πˆx(y) > 0 for all x and y. This restriction is not mentioned in Theorem 1 (the general conformal calibration result) nor in the definition of C∗(x,u,τ) in Section 2.2. It's unclear why this restriction is needed for the specific case of RAPS but not for the general conformal calibration framework. The authors should either justify why this restriction is necessary for their method or clarify that it's not actually required (perhaps it's just a simplifying assumption for the proof).

comments.title: Vague condition for upper bound on coverage
comments.quote: Roughly speaking, this will hold whenever the sets grow smoothly in τ. See Lei et al. (2018) for a formal statement of the required conditions.
comments.explanation: The authors mention an upper bound on coverage that holds when 'sets grow smoothly in τ' but don't provide any clarification about what this means. While they reference Lei et al. (2018) for formal conditions, readers would benefit from at least a brief explanation or example of what 'growing smoothly' entails in this context. This is particularly important since the upper bound is mentioned right after the main coverage guarantee.

comments.title: Strong performance claims without immediate verification
comments.quote: achieving coverage with sets that are often factors of 5 to 10 smaller than a stand-alone Platt scaling baseline. [...] However, APS sets have an average size of 19, while RAPS sets have an average size of 2 at α = 10% (Figure 2 and Table 1).
comments.explanation: The authors make strong claims about performance improvements (5-10x smaller sets, specific comparison of 19 vs 2 average size) in the Abstract and Introduction. While these claims are likely verified in the experiments section (which we don't see in this passage), making such strong quantitative claims so early in the paper without immediate supporting evidence could be seen as overstating. The claims would be better positioned with more cautious language or with immediate reference to the supporting results.

comments.title: Notation inconsistency for set-valued function
comments.explanation: The paper first defines a general set-valued function C(x,u,τ) in Theorem 1, then later introduces C*(x,u,τ) in Equation (4) for their specific RAPS method. The relationship between these isn't clearly established - is C* a specific instance of C, or does the asterisk indicate something else? This creates potential confusion since both notations are used in subsequent propositions without clarification.

comments.title: Overstated optimality claim
comments.quote: we show that RAPS is guaranteed to have better performance than choosing a fixed-size set... Proposition 2 (RAPS dominates top-k sets)
comments.explanation: The text claims RAPS is better than 'choosing a fixed-size set' generally, but Proposition 2 only proves dominance over 'top-k predictive sets' (sets containing the k most probable classes). This is a special case of fixed-size sets. The claim overgeneralizes what's actually proven, as there could be other fixed-size strategies not covered by this proposition.

comments.title: Unmotivated randomization term
comments.quote: The second term, _πx_ ( _y_ ) _· u_ , is a randomized term to handle the fact that the value will jump discretely with the inclusion of each new _y_ . The randomization term can never impact more than one value of _y_ : there is at most one value of _y_ such that _y ∈C_ ( _x,_ 0 _, τ_ ) but _y ∈C/_ ( _x,_ 1 _, τ_ ).
comments.explanation: The necessity of the randomization term π̂x(y)·u isn't well-motivated. While the authors explain what it does (handles discrete jumps), they don't explain why this is necessary for their method or what problems would occur without it. The reference to 'usual way' and deferral to Appendix B leaves readers without sufficient justification in the main text.

Deferring this discussion to Appendix E means readers cannot fully understand or implement the method without jumping sections. This is particularly problematic since these parameters crucially affect the trade-off between set size and coverage.

progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.label: Progressive (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.overall_feedback: This paper is an exemplary introduction to an important problem in modern machine learning: bridging the gap between high-accuracy deep learning models and the rigorous requirements of safety-critical decision-making. The authors present a highly credible and well-structured motivation, clearly framing the distinction between naive heuristic-based calibration and the formal, distribution-free guarantees provided by conformal prediction. The clarity of the exposition is exceptional, particularly in the way it defines the technical objective (Eq. 1) and provides an intuitive logical progression from "naive" approaches to "APS" and finally to the proposed "RAPS" modification. The primary strength lies in the authors' ability to translate rigorous theoretical guarantees into a highly practical tool with minimal computational overhead. progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Progressive (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback: This paper addresses the critical challenge of reliable uncertainty quantification for deep learning classifiers by adapting conformal prediction to generate prediction sets with formal finite-sample coverage guarantees. The work is of high quality and relevance, presenting a theoretically rigorous, black-box method that is computationally efficient and empirically effective, achieving prediction sets that are orders of magnitude smaller than naive baselines while maintaining the desired coverage on large-scale benchmarks like ImageNet. The writing is clear and compelling, effectively motivating the need for actionable uncertainty in consequential settings and logically delineating the limitations of existing calibration techniques versus the proposed Regularized Adaptive Prediction Sets (RAPS). However, the most significant issue is the reliance on marginal coverage; the authors explicitly note that guarantees hold "on average," which may be insufficient for safety-critical applications requiring conditional coverage per instance, and the paper should more deeply discuss this limitation and potential mitigation strategies. Additionally, while the method improves upon Platt scaling and APS, the evaluation should include comparisons to other modern uncertainty baselines, such as deep ensembles or Bayesian approximations, to fully contextualize the practical advantages of conformal approaches. Finally, the practical constraints regarding the size of the calibration set and the stability of the regularization for small datasets warrant a more thorough analysis to ensure robustness in real-world deployment scenarios.

comments.title: Conflated terminology: alpha defined as 'confidence level' instead of miscoverage rate
comments.quote: for a pre-specified confidence level _\u03b1_ such as 10%.
comments.explanation: In conformal prediction and statistical coverage guarantees, \u03b1 conventionally denotes the miscoverage rate (or error level), while 1−\u03b1 denotes the confidence level. The passage calls \u03b1 the 'confidence level' and gives the example 10%, which is terminologically inverted. Although the subsequent derivation (using threshold 1−\u03b1) is mathematically correct and consistent with the 90% coverage goal, this phrasing contradicts standard notation and could confuse readers about the direction of the coverage inequality. A minor terminology fix is needed.

comments.explanation: The phrase 'APSsets' is a clear missing space. The intended text is 'APS sets'. This is a straightforward formatting slip that does not impact the technical meaning but should be corrected for publication readiness.

comments.title: Overstatement in empirical comparison claim
comments.quote: We find that RAPS sets always have smaller average size than naive and APS sets.
comments.explanation: The absolute term 'always' is too strong for an experimental observation. While RAPS likely yields smaller sets than APS across their benchmarks, naive sets can sometimes be smaller (though they underperform in coverage due to miscalibrated probabilities). The authors should clarify that RAPS consistently yields smaller sets *among methods that achieve valid coverage*, or specify 'on average across our experiments' to avoid implying a theoretical guarantee.

comments.title: Ambiguous algorithmic order for probability calibration
comments.quote: (e.g., after Platt scaling and a softmax function; see Platt et al. 1999; Guo et al. 2017)
comments.explanation: The phrasing 'after Platt scaling and a softmax function' could be misread as applying Platt scaling and softmax sequentially. In practice, Platt scaling is either applied to the raw logits before the softmax, or applied directly to the softmax outputs of a pre-trained model to correct calibration. A slight rewording would improve technical precision, though the broader point about using calibrated probabilities is clear.

comments.title: Misleading notation for randomization parameter
comments.quote: there is at most one value of $y$ such that $y \in C(x, 0, \tau)$ but $y \notin C(x, 1, \tau)$.
comments.explanation: The set-valued function was previously defined as $C(x, u, \tau)$ where $u \in [0, 1]$ is a random variable used for score randomization. The text suddenly evaluates the function at arguments 0 and 1, implying deterministic boundary cases rather than a varying randomization parameter. The authors likely intended to state that varying $u$ across $[0,1]$ changes the predictive set by at most one element, or that the set is monotonic in $u$. As written, the notation contradicts the established definition.

However, the introduction and methods sections explicitly state the algorithm applies to 'any black-box classifier' and works directly on the raw classifier scores/probabilities. Presenting it as a prerequisite in the abstract overstates the method's dependencies and may mislead readers into thinking conformal calibration requires it.

Suppose that the sets are indexed by $\tau$ such that they are _nested_ , meaning larger values of $\tau$ lead to larger sets:
